texts.label: text
texts.orig: An analysis of the e/VTects of /FFber packing on mass transfer coe/SOcients for axial /CRuid/CRow through /FFber bund les with uniform wall /CRux in the well-d evelopedlimit is presented . The /FFnite element methodis usedto solve the governing momentum andconservation of mass equations. The e/VTective mass transfer coe/SOcient depends strongly on /FFber packing. Randomly packed /FFber bundles have much lower mass transfer coe/SOcients than regularly packed/FFber bund les. Mass transfer rates are controlledby the lowest /FFber packing regions through which most of the /CRow occurs. ? 2002 Elsevier Science Ltd. All rights reserved.
texts.text: An analysis of the e/VTects of /FFber packing on mass transfer coe/SOcients for axial /CRuid/CRow through /FFber bund les with uniform wall /CRux in the well-d evelopedlimit is presented . The /FFnite element methodis usedto solve the governing momentum andconservation of mass equations. The e/VTective mass transfer coe/SOcient depends strongly on /FFber packing. Randomly packed /FFber bundles have much lower mass transfer coe/SOcients than regularly packed/FFber bund les. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation processes are gaining increased acceptance in many industries. These processes include hemodialysis, gas separation, reverse osmosis and /FFltration (Ho & Sirkar, 1992).
texts.text: Membrane separation processes are gaining increased acceptance in many industries. These processes include hemodialysis, gas separation, reverse osmosis and /FFltration (Ho & Sirkar, 1992).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane processes commonly utilize membranes in the form of /FFne hollow /FFbers. A multitude of these /FFbers are grouped together to form a hollow /FFber bundle. The bundle is placedin a case andtubesheets are formedthat encapsulate both ends of the module. Machining of the tubesheets opens the /FFber lumens (/FFber interiors). Placing the bundle into a case andsealing the tubesheets to the case allows one to introd uce a /CRuidinto both the lumen andshell space (space outside the /FFbers) without mixing the two streams. The lumen /CRuidenters through the lumen port andad jacent headspaces while the shell /CRuid enters through the shell ports on the exterior of the case as illustratedin Fig. 1.
texts.text: Membrane processes commonly utilize membranes in the form of /FFne hollow /FFbers. A multitude of these /FFbers are grouped together to form a hollow /FFber bundle. The bundle is placedin a case andtubesheets are formedthat encapsulate both ends of the module. Machining of the tubesheets opens the /FFber lumens (/FFber interiors). Placing the bundle into a case andsealing the tubesheets to the case allows one to introd uce a /CRuidinto both the lumen andshell space (space outside the /FFbers) without mixing the two streams. The lumen /CRuidenters through the lumen port andad jacent headspaces while the shell /CRuid enters through the shell ports on the exterior of the case as illustratedin Fig. 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of these devices is often limited by concentration boundary layers that develop in the lumen and shell spaces. These boundary layers are in turn dependent on the nature of the shell andlumen /CRows as d ictatedby module design factors such as /FFber diameter, /FFber spacing, andinlet = outlet port geometry. The impact of lumen

texts.orig: concentration boundary layers on performance is described well in the literature (Cussler, 1997). However, less is known about shell boundary layers, because of the complex geometry of randomly packed /FFber bundles.
texts.text: concentration boundary layers on performance is described well in the literature (Cussler, 1997). However, less is known about shell boundary layers, because of the complex geometry of randomly packed /FFber bundles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Previous researchers have largely ignoredthe complex /CRows that occur in real modules when predicting mass transfer coe/SOcients. Commonly, /FFbers are assumedto be packedin regular arrays (e.g. square or triangular). Sparrow andLoe/SIer (1959) present an analytic solution for the well-d evelopedvelocity /FFeldfor axial /CRow through an in/FFnite, regular array of cylindrical rods. Noda and Gryte (1979) usedthis solution to d erive the concentration /FFelds and mass transfer coe/SOcients for axial /CRows in the well-d evelopedlimit. Miyatake andIwashita (1990, 1991) present a /FFnite di/VTerence solution for the temperature /FFelds andassociatedheat transfer coe/SOcients for laminar axial /CRow through regular arrays of cylinders using the Sparrow andLoe/SIer velocity /FFeld . Their results cover a range of /CRow rates that encompass both entry andwell-d eveloped heat transfer limits, andcan be usedto pred ict shell-sid e mass transfer in regular arrays of hollow /FFbers (Kreulen, Smolders, Versteeg, & van Swaaij, 1993).
texts.text: Previous researchers have largely ignoredthe complex /CRows that occur in real modules when predicting mass transfer coe/SOcients. Commonly, /FFbers are assumedto be packedin regular arrays (e.g. square or triangular). Sparrow andLoe/SIer (1959) present an analytic solution for the well-d evelopedvelocity /FFeldfor axial /CRow through an in/FFnite, regular array of cylindrical rods. Noda and Gryte (1979) usedthis solution to d erive the concentration /FFelds and mass transfer coe/SOcients for axial /CRows in the well-d evelopedlimit. Miyatake andIwashita (1990, 1991) present a /FFnite di/VTerence solution for the temperature /FFelds andassociatedheat transfer coe/SOcients for laminar axial /CRow through regular arrays of cylinders using the Sparrow andLoe/SIer velocity /FFeld . Their results cover a range of /CRow rates that encompass both entry andwell-d eveloped heat transfer limits, andcan be usedto pred ict shell-sid e mass transfer in regular arrays of hollow /FFbers (Kreulen, Smolders, Versteeg, & van Swaaij, 1993).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many commercial hollow /FFber modules, however, consist of randomly packed /FFber bundles. Shell /CRow maldistribution, or channeling, is often encounteredd ue to poor /FFber packing (Yang & Cussler, 1986). To calculate /CRow rates for randomly packed hollow /FFber bundles, Chen and

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hlavacek (1994) generalize the equivalent annulus approximation through the use of Voronoi tessellation: each /FFber is surrounded with a polygon surface, and the hydraulic diameter of the enclosedregion is usedin a friction factor correlation to determine /CRow rates. Rogers and Long (1997) adopt the same assumption to calculate both friction factors andmass-transfer coe/SOcients in the entry region for randomly packed /FFber bundles. This approach appears to give better estimates of module performance than assuming regular packing. Wu andChen (2000) report similar results.
texts.text: Hlavacek (1994) generalize the equivalent annulus approximation through the use of Voronoi tessellation: each /FFber is surrounded with a polygon surface, and the hydraulic diameter of the enclosedregion is usedin a friction factor correlation to determine /CRow rates. Rogers and Long (1997) adopt the same assumption to calculate both friction factors andmass-transfer coe/SOcients in the entry region for randomly packed /FFber bundles. This approach appears to give better estimates of module performance than assuming regular packing. Wu andChen (2000) report similar results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A recent contribution (Bao, Liu, & Lipscomb, 1999) provides a /FFrst principles analysis of the e/VTect of non-uniform /FFber packing on mass transfer in the entry mass transfer limit for both constant wall concentration andconstant wall /CRux boundary conditions. The results indicate that mass transfer coe/SOcients in randomly packed bundles are much lower than in regularly packedbund les. Here, we extendthis analysis to mass transfer in /FFber bundles in the well-developed limit with constant wall /CRux. The /FFnite element method (FEM) is usedto solve the governing conservation equations. The results indicate that random /FFber packing significantly reduces mass transfer coe/SOcients, much more so in the well-developed limit than in the entry limit.
texts.text: A recent contribution (Bao, Liu, & Lipscomb, 1999) provides a /FFrst principles analysis of the e/VTect of non-uniform /FFber packing on mass transfer in the entry mass transfer limit for both constant wall concentration andconstant wall /CRux boundary conditions. The results indicate that mass transfer coe/SOcients in randomly packed bundles are much lower than in regularly packedbund les. Here, we extendthis analysis to mass transfer in /FFber bundles in the well-developed limit with constant wall /CRux. The /FFnite element method (FEM) is usedto solve the governing conservation equations. The results indicate that random /FFber packing significantly reduces mass transfer coe/SOcients, much more so in the well-developed limit than in the entry limit.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Theory
texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Spatially periodic media
texts.text: 2.1. Spatially periodic media

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The /FFber bundle is modeled as an in/FFnite, spatially periodic medium formed by translating a unit cell in each spatial direction. Fig. 2 illustrates one realization of the unit cell andpart of the in/FFnite med ium it generates. The unit cell contains multiple /FFbers that lie parallel to each other andto the z -axis. Because of this alignment, a single unit cell characterizes the entire medium. The domain geometry andvalues of any /FFeldvariable at a point ( x; y ) in the unit cell are the same as at the point ( x ± il x ; y ± jl y ), where i; j =1 ; 2 ; 3 ; : : : ; and l x and l y are the unit vectors in the x and y directions, respectively.
texts.text: The /FFber bundle is modeled as an in/FFnite, spatially periodic medium formed by translating a unit cell in each spatial direction. Fig. 2 illustrates one realization of the unit cell andpart of the in/FFnite med ium it generates. The unit cell contains multiple /FFbers that lie parallel to each other andto the z -axis. Because of this alignment, a single unit cell characterizes the entire medium. The domain geometry andvalues of any /FFeldvariable at a point ( x; y ) in the unit cell are the same as at the point ( x ± il x ; y ± jl y ), where i; j =1 ; 2 ; 3 ; : : : ; and l x and l y are the unit vectors in the x and y directions, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The size of the unit cell is determined by the number of /FFbers it contains, /FFber packing pattern andpacking fraction.
texts.text: The size of the unit cell is determined by the number of /FFbers it contains, /FFber packing pattern andpacking fraction.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2. Use of a unit cell to represent an in/FFnite, randomly packed /FFber bund le. The cell is outlinedin the center, and/FFbers with centers in the unit cell have been darkened. The arrows indicate the translation of the unit cell required to produce an in/FFnite /FFber bundle. Solid thick lines on the top andbottom (d ashedthick lines on the left andright) represent periodic boundaries.
texts.text: Fig. 2. Use of a unit cell to represent an in/FFnite, randomly packed /FFber bund le. The cell is outlinedin the center, and/FFbers with centers in the unit cell have been darkened. The arrows indicate the translation of the unit cell required to produce an in/FFnite /FFber bundle. Solid thick lines on the top andbottom (d ashedthick lines on the left andright) represent periodic boundaries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fiber centers are placedon a regular gridto generate regular /FFber packings while the locations are selectedin a rand om, sequential fashion to produce random packings. All /FFbers are assumedto possess the same d imensions.
texts.text: Fiber centers are placedon a regular gridto generate regular /FFber packings while the locations are selectedin a rand om, sequential fashion to produce random packings. All /FFbers are assumedto possess the same d imensions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The random media produced by this method are representative of in/FFnite, random media only if the unit cell contains a su/SOciently large number of /FFbers. Unfortunately, available computational resources limit the size of the unit cell that can be used.
texts.text: The random media produced by this method are representative of in/FFnite, random media only if the unit cell contains a su/SOciently large number of /FFbers. Unfortunately, available computational resources limit the size of the unit cell that can be used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To determine the e/VTect of using /FFnite cells, cells of increasing size (containing from 16 to 100 /FFbers) are used to calculate mass transfer coe/SOcients for a /FFxedpacking fraction. For a given cell size (number of /FFbers in the cell), mass transfer coe/SOcients are calculatedfor several d i/VTerent cell con/FFgurations (from 5 to 10). The average value andstand ardd eviation for each cell size are compared to determine if the variations are statistically signi/FFcant. As demonstrated later, no signi/FFcant variation is observed upon increasing the cell size for su/SOciently large cells, andthe limiting value is taken as the value for an in/FFnite bundle.
texts.text: To determine the e/VTect of using /FFnite cells, cells of increasing size (containing from 16 to 100 /FFbers) are used to calculate mass transfer coe/SOcients for a /FFxedpacking fraction. For a given cell size (number of /FFbers in the cell), mass transfer coe/SOcients are calculatedfor several d i/VTerent cell con/FFgurations (from 5 to 10). The average value andstand ardd eviation for each cell size are compared to determine if the variations are statistically signi/FFcant. As demonstrated later, no signi/FFcant variation is observed upon increasing the cell size for su/SOciently large cells, andthe limiting value is taken as the value for an in/FFnite bundle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal of this work is to determine mass transfer coef/FFcients in the well-developed limit for axial /CRows through randomly packed /FFber bundles. In practice, such bundles are enclosedin cases andthe presence of the case will affect mass transfer in that portion of the bundle near the case. These wall e/VTects are not included in the analysis. One wouldnot expect them to be signi/FFcant for well-d esigned modules in which the /FFber diameter is much smaller than the bundle diameter, and they are routinely neglected when mass transfer coe/SOcients are calculatedfrom experimental data or estimated from correlations. Moreover, results obtainedin the absence of wall e/VTects provid e a performance baseline from which one might infer that wall e/VTects or other module ine/SOciencies are important.
texts.text: The goal of this work is to determine mass transfer coef/FFcients in the well-developed limit for axial /CRows through randomly packed /FFber bundles. In practice, such bundles are enclosedin cases andthe presence of the case will affect mass transfer in that portion of the bundle near the case. These wall e/VTects are not included in the analysis. One wouldnot expect them to be signi/FFcant for well-d esigned modules in which the /FFber diameter is much smaller than the bundle diameter, and they are routinely neglected when mass transfer coe/SOcients are calculatedfrom experimental data or estimated from correlations. Moreover, results obtainedin the absence of wall e/VTects provid e a performance baseline from which one might infer that wall e/VTects or other module ine/SOciencies are important.

texts.text: 2.2. Governing equations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To simplify the governing conservation equations, we make the following assumptions:
texts.text: To simplify the governing conservation equations, we make the following assumptions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Fluid/CRow through the /FFber bund le is axial, laminar, and well-developed.
texts.text: Fluid/CRow through the /FFber bund le is axial, laminar, and well-developed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Mass transfer does not change shell /CRow rates (i.e., a dilute solute is transferred from shell to lumen).
texts.text: Mass transfer does not change shell /CRow rates (i.e., a dilute solute is transferred from shell to lumen).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The wall mass /CRux is constant along the surface of each /FFber.
texts.text: The wall mass /CRux is constant along the surface of each /FFber.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Axial di/VTusive mass transfer is negligible relative to convective mass transfer.
texts.text: Axial di/VTusive mass transfer is negligible relative to convective mass transfer.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Isothermal /CRow.
texts.text: Isothermal /CRow.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. Fickian di/VTusion.
texts.text: Fickian di/VTusion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. Constant /CRuidproperties.
texts.text: Constant /CRuidproperties.

texts.orig: The solution for the concentration /FFeldis inherently three-dimensional since concentration varies both within the unit cell cross section andwith axial d istance. Miyatake andIwashita (1990, 1991) solve this problem by using /FFnite di/VTerences to discretize the solution domain. The use of backward/FFnite d i/VTerences in the z direction reduces the computational complexity by eliminating the needto solve globally for all of the /FFeldvariables: one solves for the variables in a sequence of two-dimensional cross sections by marching along the axial direction from shell inlet to outlet.
texts.text: The solution for the concentration /FFeldis inherently three-dimensional since concentration varies both within the unit cell cross section andwith axial d istance. Miyatake andIwashita (1990, 1991) solve this problem by using /FFnite di/VTerences to discretize the solution domain. The use of backward/FFnite d i/VTerences in the z direction reduces the computational complexity by eliminating the needto solve globally for all of the /FFeldvariables: one solves for the variables in a sequence of two-dimensional cross sections by marching along the axial direction from shell inlet to outlet.

texts.orig: The PDE Toolbox, as implementedin Matlab (The MathWorks Inc., Natick MA), is usedto solve Eqs. (1) and(2) with the FEM. The velocity /FFeld v is computed/FFrst and usedin the concentration /FFeldcalculation. c ∗ b and c ∗ w are calculatedfrom the results to evaluate Eq. (11).
texts.text: The PDE Toolbox, as implementedin Matlab (The MathWorks Inc., Natick MA), is usedto solve Eqs. (1) and(2) with the FEM. The velocity /FFeld v is computed/FFrst and usedin the concentration /FFeldcalculation. c ∗ b and c ∗ w are calculatedfrom the results to evaluate Eq. (11).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To discretize the unit cell, Voronoi tessellation (implementedin Matlab as the function VORONOI ) is used to construct a polygon surrounding each /FFber. A typical tessellation is illustratedin Fig. 3. The bound ary of the tessellatedregions is taken as the bound ary of the unit cell, see Fig. 3. Use of this boundary eliminates the need to identify where /FFbers cross the unit cell boundary, as seen along the right andbottom bound aries in Fig. 2, and simpli/FFes the discretization process. As in Fig. 2, the top and bottom boundaries are periodic as well as the left and right, but now the boundaries consist of a sequence of line segments along which /FFeldvariables andtheir grad ients are equal.
texts.text: To discretize the unit cell, Voronoi tessellation (implementedin Matlab as the function VORONOI ) is used to construct a polygon surrounding each /FFber. A typical tessellation is illustratedin Fig. 3. The bound ary of the tessellatedregions is taken as the bound ary of the unit cell, see Fig. 3. Use of this boundary eliminates the need to identify where /FFbers cross the unit cell boundary, as seen along the right andbottom bound aries in Fig. 2, and simpli/FFes the discretization process. As in Fig. 2, the top and bottom boundaries are periodic as well as the left and right, but now the boundaries consist of a sequence of line segments along which /FFeldvariables andtheir grad ients are equal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results for square andtriangular arrays are given in Table 1. Table 1 also contains literature values for /FFf taken from Miyatake andIwashita (1991) andDwyer andBerry (1970) for the same types of packing. Our results are in excellent agreement with the literature. The triangular array results di/VTer by ¡ 2% over the entire /FFber packing fraction range. The square array results also di/VTer by ¡ 2%for packing fractions ¡ 0 : 55 but the di/VTerence increases to 10% as the packing fraction decreases to 0.05. We attribute this to potential numerical approximation error in the calculations of Miyatake andIwashita. The overall level of agreement, however, indicated in Table 1 provides strong support for the validity of our approach.
texts.text: The results for square andtriangular arrays are given in Table 1. Table 1 also contains literature values for /FFf taken from Miyatake andIwashita (1991) andDwyer andBerry (1970) for the same types of packing. Our results are in excellent agreement with the literature. The triangular array results di/VTer by ¡ 2% over the entire /FFber packing fraction range. The square array results also di/VTer by ¡ 2%for packing fractions ¡ 0 : 55 but the di/VTerence increases to 10% as the packing fraction decreases to 0.05. We attribute this to potential numerical approximation error in the calculations of Miyatake andIwashita. The overall level of agreement, however, indicated in Table 1 provides strong support for the validity of our approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To evaluate the e/VTect of /FFber number in the unit cell, we performedcalculations using nine, 16 ; 32 ; 64 and100 /FFbers in the unit cell at 50% packing fraction. At least six di/VTerent cells were simulated for each case to evaluate mass transfer in di/VTerent realizations of the /FFber bundle. Up to 70,000 nodes were used in the calculations. Fig. 7 shows the average Sherwoodnumber variation with /FFber number. Unit cells containing fewer /FFbers give slightly higher values for Sh . Additionally, the variation between di/VTerent unit cells is generally larger for cells with fewer /FFbers. An analysis of variance (ANOVA, McBean & Rovers, 1998) of the values calculatedshows no signi/FFcant d i/VTerence between the results obtainedusing 16 ; 64 and100 /FFbers at the 95% con/FFdence level. Consequently, we used 64 /FFbers in the unit cell to perform calculations for all other /FFber packings.
texts.text: To evaluate the e/VTect of /FFber number in the unit cell, we performedcalculations using nine, 16 ; 32 ; 64 and100 /FFbers in the unit cell at 50% packing fraction. Fig. 7 shows the average Sherwoodnumber variation with /FFber number. Unit cells containing fewer /FFbers give slightly higher values for Sh . Additionally, the variation between di/VTerent unit cells is generally larger for cells with fewer /FFbers. An analysis of variance (ANOVA, McBean & Rovers, 1998) of the values calculatedshows no signi/FFcant d i/VTerence between the results obtainedusing 16 ; 64 and100 /FFbers at the 95% con/FFdence level. Consequently, we used 64 /FFbers in the unit cell to perform calculations for all other /FFber packings.

texts.orig: respectively. Fig. 8 indicates that /CRow through the randomly packed/FFber bund le can be highly non-uniform: signi/FFcant channeling exists through regions where the /FFber packing is lowest while the highest /FFber packing regions are nearly stagnant. Concentrations are higher in the high packing = low /CRow regions because of the constant wall /CRux boundary cond ition (mass is transferredinto the /CRuidfrom the /FFber). Concentrations are lower in the low packing = high /CRow regions andmass transfer coe/SOcients are lower. Overall performance is dominated by the high /CRow regions which leads to a large reduction in mass transfer coe/SOcient relative to a regular /FFber packing.
texts.text: respectively. Concentrations are lower in the low packing = high /CRow regions andmass transfer coe/SOcients are lower. texts.orig: Fig. 10 illustrates the dependence of /FFf on /FFber packing fraction for regular andrand omly packedbund les. Fig. 10 clearly indicates mass transfer coe/SOcients for random packings are much smaller than for regular packings. Values for random packing are only 5-10% of the values for regular packing. Furthermore, mass transfer coe/SOcients are strongly dependent on the /FFber packing fraction for regular packing whereas for random packing, the dependence is much weaker. The deviation between random and regular packing decreases in the low and high packing limits.

Furthermore, mass transfer coe/SOcients are strongly dependent on the /FFber packing fraction for regular packing whereas for random packing, the dependence is much weaker. The deviation between random and regular packing decreases in the low and high packing limits.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A theoretical analysis of mass transfer in the well-developedlimit for axial /CRows through rand omly packed/FFber bundles with constant wall /CRux is presented. In/FFnite randomly packed bundles are generated by randomly placing a /FFnite number of /FFbers in a unit cell andtranslating the unit cell along each spatial coordinate. The conservation of momentum andmass equations are solvedto calculate overall mass transfer coe/SOcients using the /FFnite element method.
texts.text: A theoretical analysis of mass transfer in the well-developedlimit for axial /CRows through rand omly packed/FFber bundles with constant wall /CRux is presented. In/FFnite randomly packed bundles are generated by randomly placing a /FFnite number of /FFbers in a unit cell andtranslating the unit cell along each spatial coordinate. The conservation of momentum andmass equations are solvedto calculate overall mass transfer coe/SOcients using the /FFnite element method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mass transfer coe/SOcients for regular square andtriangular arrays are in goodagreement with literature values. Results for random /FFber packing indicate that mass transfer coe/SOcients decrease dramatically in the well-developed limit. This drop is due to high /CRows through low /FFber packing regions (i.e. channeling).
texts.text: Mass transfer coe/SOcients for regular square andtriangular arrays are in goodagreement with literature values. Results for random /FFber packing indicate that mass transfer coe/SOcients decrease dramatically in the well-developed limit. This drop is due to high /CRows through low /FFber packing regions (i.e. channeling).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results clearly indicate that randomly packed /FFber bund les shouldnot be usedfor applications that operate near the low Graetz number regime. Adding structure to the bundle by weaving /FFbers or other means can dramatically increase mass transfer coe/SOcients.
texts.text: The results clearly indicate that randomly packed /FFber bund les shouldnot be usedfor applications that operate near the low Graetz number regime. Adding structure to the bundle by weaving /FFbers or other means can dramatically increase mass transfer coe/SOcients.
